title: WordPiece: Likelihood Merging
concept: WordPiece chooses merges based on how much they increase the likelihood of the training data under a unigram model, commonly used in BERT.

exercises.explain: WordPiece is a subword tokenization algorithm that helps language models split text into meaningful chunks.

cards.title: Smart Merging
cards.body: How does an AI like BERT decide which parts of words belong together? Instead of just counting the most common pairs, it uses a clever statistical test to find pairs that are 'meant to be' together!

cards.title: What is WordPiece Likelihood Merging?
cards.body: Unlike BPE which merges the most frequent pairs, WordPiece decides what to merge based on **likelihood**. It asks: 'If we merge these two pieces, how much better does it explain our training text?' It prioritizes pairs that appear together much more often than we would expect by chance. This algorithm is famously used by models like BERT.

cards.title: The Star Duo Analogy
cards.body: Think of a popular music duo. If you see the word 'Beyoncé' and 'Jay-Z' appear together constantly, it is not a random coincidence. Their joint appearance is way more frequent than you would expect if they just randomly bumped into each other. WordPiece looks for pairs of subwords that form these 'super-groups' rather than just pairing letters that happen to be common on their own.

cards.title: WordPiece in Action
cards.body: Suppose we have the subwords 'un' and 'believable'. Even if 'un' is common and 'believable' is somewhat common, they appear together as 'unbelievable' way more often than chance would predict. WordPiece detects this high-likelihood pairing and merges them into a single token, helping models like BERT understand the word as a whole.

cards.title: BPE vs. WordPiece
cards.body: You might think WordPiece and Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) are exactly the same. While they both build vocabularies by merging pieces, BPE simply counts frequency, whereas WordPiece uses statistical likelihood. This makes WordPiece a bit more selective about what it merges!

cards.title: BPE vs. WordPiece Merging
cards.body: Here is a comparison of how BPE and WordPiece decide on their next vocabulary merge.

left.text: Looks at raw counts. Merges whatever pair shows up the most times, even if they are just common individual letters.

right.text: Looks at likelihood. Merges pairs that appear together much more often than expected by individual probability.

exercises.prompt: Which famous AI language model is well-known for using the WordPiece tokenization algorithm?
exercises.explain: WordPiece is the default tokenizer family used in the training of the classic BERT model.

exercises.prompt: True or False: WordPiece and BPE use the exact same mathematical formula to decide which subwords to merge.
exercises.explain: BPE merges by raw frequency, whereas WordPiece merges based on likelihood.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence about WordPiece's merging strategy.
exercises.sentence: Instead of raw frequency, WordPiece prioritizes merges that maximize training data ________.
exercises.explain: WordPiece uses training data likelihood to decide which subwords to combine, whereas BPE uses raw frequency.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI systems is describing the WordPiece merging strategy?

options.text: AI A: 'Let's count how many times "in" and "g" appear next to each other and merge them because they have the highest raw count.'
AI B: 'Let's check if merging "un" and "safe" makes our overall training data significantly more likely than keeping them separate.'
AI C: 'Let's randomly group letters together until we reach a vocabulary limit of 10,000 words.'

exercises.explain: Option A represents BPE (raw count). Option B represents WordPiece, which looks at how much the combination improves the model's likelihood compared to the parts.

exercises.prompt: How does the WordPiece algorithm decide which two subwords to merge next?

options.text: It merges the pair that has the absolute highest raw frequency in the text.
It merges the pair that increases the training data likelihood the most.
It merges characters randomly to ensure diversity in the vocabulary.
It only merges words that are misspelled in the training data.

exercises.explain: WordPiece evaluates potential merges by checking how much more likely the training data is when the two parts are merged, rather than kept separate. This maximizes the overall training data likelihood.
